original_query: select * from (values (30, 3)) as t (x, y) intersect select * from (values (10, 1), (30, 3)) as t (x, y) where x > 50 intersect select * from (values (30, 3)) as t (x, y);
rewritten_query: SELECT * FROM (VALUES (30, 3)) AS t(x, y) WHERE FALSE;

rewrite_suggestion.produced_suggestion: The original query contains a WHERE clause 'x > 50' in the second subquery that filters out all rows since the maximum x value is 30. This makes the second subquery return an empty result set.

Since INTERSECT returns rows that appear in all result sets, and one of the result sets is empty, the entire query will return no rows. This can be simplified to a query that explicitly returns no rows using 'WHERE FALSE'.

The database might not optimize away the impossible condition in the second subquery, leading to unnecessary processing. By rewriting to a simple query with 'WHERE FALSE', we eliminate all the unnecessary operations.
